title: Distinguished Eng On Stack Ranking, Competing with Bezos, Regrets | Bryan Cantrill
guest_role: Co-founder/CTO of Oxide Computer, ex-Sun Distinguished Engineer, ex-Joyent CTO

sections.title: Why goaling on promotion is bad
Why he hated the Oracle acquisition
Why Bezos is the apex predator of capitalism
When he was happiest in his career

key_takeaways: Cantrill never goaled on rank: 'I would much rather be under promoted than over promoted.' Promotions happened as a byproduct of doing the most important work, and his career highlights were hard bugs and 'impossible' projects, never the promos themselves.

Sun's rating system forced a promotion whenever a 'superlative' grade was given, so managers apologetically withheld the top grade because promos were rationed to every 18 months; formalized annual performance review 'never resulted in me having higher performance.'

Stack ranking ('rank and yank') is 'organizational cancer': firing a bottom percentage incentivizes managers to keep dead weight as sacrificial fodder — which he saw at Sun — and it teaches you that your team are adversaries when it's teams that do extraordinary things. Oxide has no ranks at all because 'ranks are corrosive.'

Sun's Distinguished Engineer promotion was a political 'country club' vote by existing DEs, with a side door for acqui-hired CTOs; Cantrill only escaped the politics because Sun's CTO Greg Papadopoulos personally presented his case and it passed unanimously.

To engineers goaling on titles or financial independence he prescribes finding meaning instead: he can introduce you to miserable Distinguished Engineers and miserable wealthy people, dot-com winners who cashed out often floundered for a decade, and even meaningful shipped goals produce 'postpartum in engineering.'

After the dot-com bust Sun lost 98% of its value (trading below cash), ran roughly 35 rounds of layoffs over eight years, and engineers scripted the employee directory into a daily 'obits' report to see who was gone; McNealy's delay in starting layoffs was a mistake.

He quit Oracle about 45 days after the acquisition closed because he was ashamed to work there: Sun had its customers' trust and Oracle — 'an octopus that knows how to feed itself' — does not; meanwhile other Sun engineers lobbied to get on the layoff list for the rich severance.

Bezos is 'the apex predator of capitalism': Amazon hid AWS's great margins by refusing to break out revenue and relentlessly cut prices at every re:Invent from 2010-2015, convincing the world the cloud was a bad business so nobody would enter — while Joyent, competing directly, knew the margins were excellent.

Oxide's founding lesson: a famous VC who had emailed 'I will fund literally anything you put in front of me' refused the computer-company pitch in 45 seconds, but when a VC asks 'how can I help?' you should have a concrete answer — Cantrill asked for an intro to a failed similar startup's founder and learned it died from a tiny real market plus premature go-to-market scaling. Oxide's MVP was a full rack that took 3+ years; they deliberately neither compete nor partner with Nvidia, focusing on general-purpose compute.

Boom/bust advice for the AI era: cycles are endemic to software, so be your own judge — people said operating systems were done in 1996 and that you couldn't start a computer company in 2019, and he's glad he ignored them; don't pick a space just because it's hot.

notable_quotes.speaker: Bryan Cantrill
notable_quotes.quote: Stack ranking is organizational cancer... fundamentally, stack ranking teaches you that your team are adversaries.

notable_quotes.speaker: Bryan Cantrill
notable_quotes.quote: I would much rather be under promoted than over promoted... I am not interested in doing work performatively.

notable_quotes.speaker: Bryan Cantrill
notable_quotes.quote: For Sun, I was embarrassed from time to time, but never ashamed. And with Oracle, I was ashamed.

notable_quotes.speaker: Bryan Cantrill
notable_quotes.quote: He is not merely a predator, he is an apex predator in capitalism... I'm gonna do it honestly, the right way — by giving a great product at a reasonable price.
